title: From question to source

objectives.statement: Classify the answer product a knowledge need requires and justify an appropriate starting source type or discovery environment.
Convert a natural-language knowledge need into two to four searchable concept blocks with meaning-preserving term variants.
Use a query translation or small result sample to revise one concept block while preserving the boundary between retrieval and appraisal.

scenes.title: A search box is not an answer box
scenes.contentHtml:
A search box is not an answer box

Imagine that you need evidence for a laboratory discussion tomorrow. Typing the whole question into the first search box you see may return many plausible-looking items, yet still leave you unsure whether you found an overview, a study, a recommendation, or merely a list of citations.

This lesson gives you a decision chain for that earlier stage of research:

knowledge need → answer product → starting source → concept blocks → revision signal

By the end, you will be able to justify a starting source, turn a natural-language need into searchable concepts, and revise a first prototype without treating retrieval as evidence appraisal.

Scope

You will work at a foundation level with short biomedical and education examples. You will not yet judge source authority, construct a comprehensive reproducible database strategy, read a paper critically, or synthesize findings. Those tasks belong to later outcomes. This lesson also does not provide personal medical advice or authorize a clinical decision.

Callout

Every health-related case in this lesson is fictional and exists only to practise information seeking. Do not use a classroom search plan to diagnose, select treatment, or replace a qualified professional or information specialist.

Accessibility and alternatives

All required relationships appear in text and linear tables. You may draft concept blocks with a keyboard, on paper, or by dictating to a scribe. No task depends on color, position, sound, motion, dragging, a time limit, personal health information, or access to a paid database. In print, read each table from its header row downward.

scenes.title: What are you actually trying to find?
scenes.contentHtml:
What are you actually trying to find?

This check is ungraded. Use it to choose your route through the lesson.

Consider this fictional need:

I need to prepare a short explanation of whether retrieval practice helps university biology learners remember material after a delay.

Before reading further, write brief answers to three prompts.

  1. What form should the answer take: a first orientation, a synthesis of several studies, one original study, a recommendation, or a set of candidate citations?
  2. Which two to four ideas would a relevant item have to represent?
  3. Is “PubMed” a document type, a search service, or both?

Route from your attempt

  • If you can state an answer product, name at least two necessary ideas, and distinguish a document from a service that locates documents, continue to the next scene.
  • If any prompt is blocked, use the recovery route below. A gap here is routing information, not failure.

Recovery route

Rewrite the need as two lines:

I need [kind of answer] about [subject or population] and [process, exposure, or intervention]
in order to [use of the answer].

Then underline nouns or short noun phrases that determine whether an item would be relevant. Put each different idea in its own pair of brackets. Do not worry about database syntax. Carry the bracketed draft into the next scene and compare it with the decision table there.

scenes.title: Match the answer product to a starting source
scenes.contentHtml:
Match the answer product to a starting source

A knowledge need states what you need to know and why. An answer product states what form of information would satisfy that need now. Naming the product first prevents a category error such as expecting a citation database to act like a reviewed recommendation.

A functional starting map

This table is a starting heuristic, not a ranking of trustworthiness.

Answer product needed now Plausible starting source Function of that source Boundary to preserve
Orientation to established terms and relationships A current textbook, handbook, or reference work Organizes principles and definitions for consultation Check date, scope, and later evidence rather than treating an overview as the final word
A synthesis of a defined body of research A systematic review or another explicit evidence synthesis Uses stated methods to identify and combine evidence relevant to a question The review's eligibility criteria and date determine what it can cover
Original methods, observations, and results A primary research report, such as a clinical or observational study report Reports one investigation rather than primarily combining prior studies One study cannot automatically establish a general conclusion
A recommendation for a setting or jurisdiction A current guideline from the responsible organization Gives directions or principles for decisions within a declared scope Confirm population, jurisdiction, issuer, and date before applying it
Candidate records from the biomedical literature A bibliographic database such as PubMed Searches citation records and publication types using the concepts supplied Retrieval and ranking do not establish individual article quality

Bookshelf and PubMed illustrate why the rows must not be collapsed. Bookshelf is an archive that contains several document types, including reference works, systematic reviews, and guidelines. PubMed is a discovery environment that makes literature records searchable. The container or search service is not the same thing as the document type it holds or locates.

Make the choice explicit

Use three sentences:

  1. “I need a [answer product] because I must [use] the answer.”
  2. “I will start with [source type or discovery environment] because its function is [function].”
  3. “Before using any candidate, I still need to check [scope, authority, date, methods, or quality].”

If you need two products, use two routes. For example, a synthesis of effects and a current jurisdiction-specific recommendation are not interchangeable outputs.

Callout

This lesson teaches functional matching only. PREM-02.02 will add source hierarchy and authority; PREM-02.03 will add reproducible database searching and inclusion criteria.

Source Note
claims claim-source-function-match, claim-database-not-appraisalsources source-pubmed-user-guide, source-bookshelf-overview, source-nlm-publication-types, source-pubmed-disclaimer

The NLM publication-type scope notes and Bookshelf overview support the bounded source functions. The PubMed guide and disclaimer support the distinction between finding records and judging individual items.

scenes.title: Turn prose into concept blocks
scenes.contentHtml:
Turn prose into concept blocks

After choosing an answer product, translate the need into ideas a source can represent. PubMed's introductory guidance begins with the key concepts, and its current interface can translate untagged terms through automatic term mapping. The purpose here is to plan concepts, not to build a comprehensive database strategy.

Remove the narrative wrapping

Start with the diagnostic need:

Does retrieval practice help university biology learners remember material after a delay?

Ask of each phrase: Would changing this idea change which items count as relevant? A useful first draft is:

[university biology learners]
[retrieval practice]
[delayed recall]

The lesson uses two to four blocks as a manageable drafting heuristic. It is not a PubMed rule. Keep a comparison such as rereading only if the question truly requires that comparison; adding every detail can make the prototype too narrow.

Generate variants without changing the idea

A keyword is an ordinary word or phrase used for retrieval. A controlled vocabulary supplies maintained subject terms; Medical Subject Headings (MeSH) is NLM's example for describing articles. Candidate variants belong inside one block only when they might express the same intended idea.

Concept block Candidate ordinary-language variants to test What would require a new block?
University biology learners undergraduate biology students; college biology learners A different population that changes eligibility
Retrieval practice practice testing; retrieval-based learning A different learning activity
Delayed recall long-term retention; later recall A different outcome, such as learner satisfaction

These are author-created candidates, not certified synonyms. A database's vocabulary and the retrieved records must confirm or reject them.

Treat the first query as a diagnostic prototype

PubMed may modify or add terms and shows its interpretation in Search Details. After a small result sample, revise one block at a time:

  • too few plausible candidates: remove an unnecessary constraint or test another expression for the same concept;
  • many off-target candidates: replace a vague expression with a more specific one;
  • a concept is missing from the translation or titles: repair that block before changing the others.

A result count is not a mastery score and a translation is not a quality judgment. The prototype tells you how the system interpreted your plan. Formal documentation and comprehensive validation are deferred to PREM-02.03.

Source Note
claims claim-key-concepts-first, claim-terms-and-vocabulary, claim-query-is-prototypesources source-pubmed-user-guide, source-nlm-mesh-course

The current PubMed guide supports identifying key concepts, testing alternative terms, inspecting Search Details, and revising overly broad or narrow retrieval. NLM's MeSH course supports the controlled-vocabulary description and its limits.

scenes.title: Work from a study need to a search prototype
scenes.contentHtml:
Work from a study need to a search prototype

This complete model uses a fictional learner and a constructed result sample. The sample is instructional, not a claim about a live PubMed search.

Worked Example
id worked-example-question-to-source

Task. A learner needs a current synthesis addressing whether retrieval practice improves delayed recall among university biology learners. They must produce a defensible starting plan, not a final evidence conclusion.

Represent the givens and unknowns.

Element Representation
Use Prepare a short study-method briefing
Known subject Retrieval practice in university biology learning
Needed outcome Delayed recall or retention
Unknown Which sources synthesize the relevant evidence
Boundary No personal clinical decision; no claim that retrieved items are high quality

Select the model. The needed answer product combines findings across studies, so a systematic review is a plausible document type. PubMed is a plausible discovery environment for candidate biomedical and health-science records. This choice follows source function, not a universal source hierarchy.

Plan. Extract the minimum discriminating concepts, draft meaning-preserving variants, make a simple prototype, inspect its translation and a small sample, then revise one block.

Execute.

  1. Remove the narrative purpose phrase “prepare a short briefing.” It affects use, not topical relevance.
  2. Draft three blocks: [university biology learners] [retrieval practice] [delayed recall].
  3. Add candidates inside the blocks: [university biology learners | undergraduate biology students] [retrieval practice | practice testing] [delayed recall | long-term retention].
  4. Start with the simplest expression of one term from each block. Do not yet claim this is a comprehensive or reproducible query.
  5. Inspect the database's translation. In the constructed rehearsal, all three ideas are represented, but the result sample contains few plausible syntheses and several relevant-looking titles use broader population wording such as “undergraduate students.”
  6. Revise only the population block to test [university learners | undergraduate students]. Keep “biology” as a later eligibility check rather than an initial required term.

Check the plan independently. The answer product and source function now match. Every retained block can change relevance. The revision responds to an observed wording mismatch rather than random trial and error. The relaxed population block may improve recall but may also retrieve studies outside biology, so the learner records that trade-off. Because the question crosses biomedical and education literature, PubMed is only a prototype environment; a comprehensive later search would also require an education-focused database or an information specialist.

Interpretation. The revised plan can locate candidate reviews. It cannot establish that a review is current, methodologically sound, applicable to biology learners, or suitable for a recommendation. Those are appraisal questions.

Self-explanation. Which decision mattered most: choosing PubMed, naming the answer product, or adding a synonym? Explain why changing that decision would alter the route.

Source Note
claims claim-key-concepts-first, claim-source-function-match, claim-terms-and-vocabulary, claim-query-is-prototype, claim-database-not-appraisalsources source-pubmed-user-guide, source-bookshelf-overview, source-nlm-publication-types, source-nlm-mesh-course, source-pubmed-disclaimer

The example independently applies the source functions, key-concept approach, term mapping, iterative search inspection, and retrieval-versus-appraisal boundary documented by the named NLM/NCBI sources.

scenes.title: A retrieved record is not a vetted answer
scenes.contentHtml:
A retrieved record is not a vetted answer

Commit before reading the repair

A PubMed result appears first under Best Match. Which conclusion is warranted?

A. NLM judged it the highest-quality article.

B. The ranking system estimated that it was relevant to the query, but the article still requires appraisal.

C. It must be a systematic review.

Write A, B, or C and one reason before continuing.

Misconception
id misconception-ranking-equals-quality

The tempting model. “First result” sounds like “best evidence,” so ranking position is treated as an endorsement or quality score.

What that model cannot explain. PubMed describes Best Match as a relevance-ranking process. Separately, NLM states that inclusion in PubMed, PubMed Central, or Bookshelf is not endorsement and that it does not judge the quality of each individual item after database selection.

Repair. A discovery system answers Which records appear relevant to this query? Appraisal asks How trustworthy, applicable, current, and fit for purpose is this item? Those questions require different evidence and different steps.

Compare on the same case. Option B is warranted. Option A confuses relevance ranking with appraisal. Option C assigns a document type that ranking position alone cannot establish.

Recheck in a new case. A guideline appears in Bookshelf. Does its presence prove that it applies in your jurisdiction today? No. Its document type may match a recommendation need, but issuer, scope, date, development methods, and authority still require checking.

Source Note
claims claim-source-function-match, claim-database-not-appraisalsources source-pubmed-user-guide, source-bookshelf-overview, source-nlm-publication-types, source-pubmed-disclaimer

The PubMed guide describes relevance ranking, while the NLM disclaimer states the non-endorsement and individual-quality boundary. The other sources support the container-versus-document distinction used in the recheck.

scenes.title: Reconstruct the decision chain
scenes.contentHtml:
Reconstruct the decision chain

Close or cover the earlier scenes. From memory, complete this chain with a question for each step:

knowledge need → ______ → starting source → ______ → revision signal

Then explain why a highly ranked record is not the final answer.

Feedback after your attempt

The missing steps are answer product and concept blocks. A complete reconstruction asks:

  1. What do I need to know, and what will I do with the answer?
  2. What answer product is needed now?
  3. Which source type or discovery environment performs that function?
  4. Which distinct concepts determine relevance?
  5. What did the translation and small sample show, and which one block should change?

The ranking explanation should distinguish retrieval relevance from appraisal. If you named only keywords, return to the source-purpose scene. If you named a source but could not justify its function, rewrite the three source-choice sentences. If you changed several blocks at once, return to the prototype rule and specify one diagnostic signal.

Callout

Delay the next reconstruction. Before beginning PREM-02.02, retrieve the difference between a document type and a discovery environment. Before PREM-02.03, reproduce the five-step chain and one revision rule without notes.

Source Note
claims claim-key-concepts-first, claim-source-function-match, claim-query-is-prototype, claim-database-not-appraisalsources source-pubmed-user-guide, source-bookshelf-overview, source-nlm-publication-types, source-pubmed-disclaimer

These sources support the source-function, key-concept, query-inspection, and non-appraisal boundaries reconstructed here.

scenes.title: Fade the supports
scenes.contentHtml:
Fade the supports

Attempt each stage before reading its feedback. The support decreases; the final case leaves the decisions to you.

Stage 1: completion problem

Need: Find a synthesis of studies about whether short pre-laboratory quizzes improve practical performance among first-year biology learners.

Given: Answer product = systematic review. Blocks = [first-year biology learners] [pre-laboratory quizzes] [____].

Supply the missing block and name a plausible discovery environment.

Feedback. The missing idea is practical laboratory performance, expressed with a candidate such as practical performance or laboratory skill performance. PubMed could be a starting discovery environment for candidate biomedical-education records. If you added “systematic review” as a topical block, separate document type from subject concepts and use the document type as a later filter or eligibility criterion.

Stage 2: supported problem

Need: Locate original methods and results from studies that measured how microscopy-image practice affects cell-identification accuracy in undergraduate biology.

Use these prompts:

  1. What answer product reports original methods and results?
  2. Which source or discovery environment can locate candidates?
  3. What three concepts determine relevance?
  4. If the sample is dominated by medical-residency studies, which block should change first?

Feedback. A primary research report matches the answer product. A bibliographic database can locate candidates. Defensible blocks represent undergraduate biology learners, microscopy-image practice, and cell-identification accuracy. If residency studies dominate, make the population block more specific while leaving the other blocks stable. The next action is to inspect whether the revised expression improves relevance, not to assume the retrieved studies are sound.

Stage 3: independent problem

A course designer needs a current orientation to cognitive load before deciding which narrower research question to ask. Construct the answer-product statement, select a source type, draft two to four concept blocks, and specify one signal that would make you revise a block.

Feedback criteria. A current textbook, handbook, or other reference work is a defensible orientation source. The concepts must represent cognitive load and the relevant learning context without smuggling “best,” “effective,” or the intended use into topical blocks. A revision signal must point to a missing or overbroad concept, not merely a disappointing count.

Stage 4: mixed selection

For each need, choose the best starting product or environment and justify the function.

Need Choose from
Learn established vocabulary before narrowing a question reference work; primary report; bibliographic database
Combine evidence from multiple eligible studies systematic review; guideline; one primary report
Inspect the methods used in one new experiment primary report; textbook; guideline
Find a dated recommendation issued for a specified setting guideline; bibliographic database; uncontrolled web explanation
Generate a set of candidate citation records bibliographic database; systematic review; guideline

Feedback. The functional matches are reference work, systematic review, primary report, guideline, and bibliographic database. If you selected the database for every row, you chose a container or discovery route where the need asked for a document product.

Retry and remediation

On a retry, use a new context rather than copying these answers. Diagnose the error first:

  • wrong product: restate “I need ___ in order to ___”;
  • wrong source function: compare the source-purpose table;
  • missing concept: underline what changes eligibility;
  • meaning-changing variant: split it into a new block or remove it;
  • too many constraints: remove one nonessential block;
  • quality claim from ranking: return to the misconception repair.
Source Note
claims claim-key-concepts-first, claim-source-function-match, claim-terms-and-vocabulary, claim-query-is-prototype, claim-database-not-appraisalsources source-pubmed-user-guide, source-bookshelf-overview, source-nlm-publication-types, source-nlm-mesh-course, source-pubmed-disclaimer

The practice cases apply the same independently synthesized, source-supported distinctions as the concept and worked-example scenes.

scenes.title: Transfer the method to a public-health brief
scenes.contentHtml:
Transfer the method to a public-health brief

A fictional regional public-health team must prepare a dated briefing on text-message reminders for adolescent vaccination appointments. It needs both a synthesis of intervention evidence and any current recommendation governing official reminder messages in its jurisdiction. Your task is to design the search route, not to recommend care.

Without copying the worked example, produce:

  1. two answer-product statements, one for evidence and one for recommendations;
  2. a starting document type or discovery route for each product;
  3. two to four concept blocks for the evidence question;
  4. the jurisdiction and date checks for the recommendation route;
  5. one translation or result-sample signal and the single block you would revise;
  6. one sentence preserving the retrieval-versus-appraisal boundary.

Self-check after attempting

A defensible response separates a systematic review from a current jurisdiction-specific guideline rather than forcing both needs into one source. The evidence blocks preserve the population, reminder intervention, appointment context, and attendance outcome; reasonable answers may combine or defer a block if the choice is justified. The recommendation route names issuer, jurisdiction, population, and currency as checks. A valid revision changes one block in response to an observed mismatch. The boundary sentence says that located candidates still require authority, methods, relevance, and quality appraisal.

This is genuine transfer because the task coordinates two answer products and adds jurisdiction and currency constraints absent from the worked example.

Callout

Do not infer a vaccination recommendation, communication policy, or expected effect from this fictional task. A real public-health brief requires current authorized guidance, domain expertise, and full evidence appraisal.

Source Note
claims claim-key-concepts-first, claim-source-function-match, claim-terms-and-vocabulary, claim-query-is-prototype, claim-database-not-appraisalsources source-pubmed-user-guide, source-bookshelf-overview, source-nlm-publication-types, source-nlm-mesh-course, source-pubmed-disclaimer

The transfer task applies the documented source functions, concept planning, vocabulary and query-inspection features, and appraisal boundary without asserting a substantive public-health conclusion.

scenes.title: Carry a search plan forward
scenes.contentHtml:
Carry a search plan forward

Use the five decisions as a compact handoff record:

Decision Record Failure signal Next action
Knowledge need What must be known and why The use is vague or unlimited Rewrite “I need ___ in order to ___”
Answer product Orientation, synthesis, original report, recommendation, or candidate citations The selected source cannot produce that form Change the product or starting source
Starting source Document type or discovery environment plus functional justification Container and document type are confused Name each separately
Concept blocks Two to four relevance-determining ideas with candidate variants An essential idea is missing or a variant changes meaning Repair one block
Revision signal Translation or sample observation tied to one change Several changes are made with no diagnosis Restore the prototype and change one block

The chain is iterative rather than magical: a source choice can reveal that the answer product was poorly stated, and a result sample can reveal that a concept was missing. The fixed boundary is that retrieval produces candidates, not a quality judgment.

Mastery evidence

Complete the open assessment only after you can:

  • justify a source by the answer product it supplies;
  • construct concept blocks without turning every sentence word into a term;
  • revise a prototype from an observed signal; and
  • state why ranking, inclusion, or full-text availability does not settle appraisal.

Retakes use changed contexts and are untimed. If one objective remains weak, practise only that decision with a new knowledge need before retaking the integrated item.

Delayed retrieval

Before PREM-02.02, retrieve the difference between source function and source authority. Before PREM-02.03, reconstruct the five-step chain, then use the later lesson to add documented syntax, controlled vocabulary, and inclusion criteria. This lesson supplies the conceptual plan; it does not claim to supply a comprehensive search.

Recovery route

Return to the row matching your error, take its single next action, and test the repaired decision in a different context. If your need is high stakes, complex, rare, interdisciplinary, or still unproductive after one reasoned revision, pause and consult an information specialist or responsible domain professional rather than widening the search indefinitely.

Source Note
claims claim-key-concepts-first, claim-source-function-match, claim-terms-and-vocabulary, claim-query-is-prototype, claim-database-not-appraisalsources source-pubmed-user-guide, source-bookshelf-overview, source-nlm-publication-types, source-nlm-mesh-course, source-pubmed-disclaimer

The handoff record synthesizes the source-supported decisions and preserves the lesson's stated heuristic, interface, and appraisal limits.

aiAssistance.disclosure: A fresh isolated OpenAI Codex authoring run copied the preserved local draft, verified its target and sources, then designed, drafted, and self-audited this candidate under the accountable principal VSBDev. This is author provenance, not independent review, approval, or evidence of learner effectiveness.

assessment.title: From question to source mastery check

items.prompt: A learner needs a document that explicitly identifies and combines evidence from several eligible studies. Which starting document type best matches that answer product?

answer.summary: A systematic review matches the requested synthesis product.
answer.reasoning: The answer product must combine evidence from several eligible studies using explicit methods. A bibliographic database can locate candidate records but is not itself the requested synthesis; one primary report supplies one investigation.
commonErrors: Choosing the database confuses a discovery environment with the document product.
Choosing one primary report does not satisfy the need to synthesize several studies.

items.prompt: For the need ‘Do short pre-laboratory quizzes improve practical performance among first-year biology learners?’, select the three ideas that should begin as distinct topical concept blocks.

answer.summary: The population, learning activity, and outcome determine topical relevance.
answer.reasoning: Changing any of the three correct ideas changes which records are relevant. ‘Best evidence’ is an appraisal demand rather than a topical concept, and ‘for my report’ states the use of the answer rather than the subject.
commonErrors: Selecting ‘best evidence’ confuses appraisal with topical retrieval.
Selecting the purpose phrase turns narrative wrapping into a concept block.
Omitting the outcome allows records that study the quizzes without measuring practical performance.

items.prompt: A record appears first under PubMed Best Match. What is the strongest warranted conclusion at this stage?

options.text: It is a candidate estimated to be relevant to the query and still requires appraisal.
NLM judged it to be the highest-quality available evidence.
It is necessarily a current guideline.
It can be used as the final answer without checking methods or scope.

answer.summary: Ranking supports retrieval relevance, not an individual quality judgment.
answer.reasoning: PubMed ranking helps order records for a query. NLM does not endorse or judge the quality of each individual included item, so document type, methods, authority, scope, and currency still require appraisal.
commonErrors: Treating ranking as quality repeats the repaired misconception.
Inferring document type or currency from rank adds information that retrieval does not supply.

items.prompt: A university accessibility team needs (1) a synthesis of research on whether captioned instructional video affects comprehension for undergraduate science learners and (2) the institution's current accessibility requirements. Write a bounded search plan that separates the two answer products, justifies a starting source or discovery route for each, gives two to four concept blocks for the research question, names one observable signal and one corresponding block revision, and preserves the retrieval-versus-appraisal boundary.

answer.correct: Separate a systematic-review route from a current institutional-policy or official-guidance route; represent undergraduate science learners, captioned instructional video, and comprehension as concepts; revise one block from a translation or result-sample mismatch; state that every candidate still requires appraisal.
answer.summary: A complete response coordinates two products and makes each revision diagnostic.
answer.reasoning: The research synthesis and current requirements perform different functions and may come from different authorities. Topical blocks determine relevance for the research route. A defensible revision changes one block in response to an observed mismatch, while appraisal remains necessary for methods, authority, applicability, and currency.
acceptableResponses: A broader evidence-synthesis type is acceptable if the learner states its explicit methods and scope.
Reasoned combinations of population and context blocks are acceptable when no essential idea is lost.
The requirements route may start at an official institutional policy register or the responsible jurisdictional authority if issuer and date are checked.
commonErrors: Using one database as both answer products without naming the documents or authorities needed.
Adding purpose words such as ‘best’ or ‘for our policy’ as topical blocks.
Changing several blocks without linking the change to a translation or sample signal.
Treating a retrieved policy or article as current and trustworthy without appraisal.

criteria.description: Selects the document type whose function matches the requested synthesis product.
Selects the measured-outcome concept and excludes narrative or appraisal phrases.
Interprets ranking as a relevance aid and preserves the need for appraisal.
Separates and justifies the two answer products and their starting routes.
Constructs two to four concept blocks that preserve the research question's essential ideas.
Links one observable translation or result-sample signal to one reasoned block revision.
States the remaining authority, methods, applicability, currency, and quality appraisal boundary.

sources.locator: Database scope, selection, quality-review, liability, and endorsement sections

agentAccess.notes: NCBI policy and PubMed robots directives were checked before substantive access. The disclaimer path was not disallowed and no agent-ingestion prohibition was found.

claims.statement: PubMed's basic search guidance begins by identifying the key concepts in an information need and entering terms for those concepts.
claims.scope: Basic topic searching in the PubMed interface as documented in the May 7, 2026 PubMed User Guide.
claims.uncertainty: The guide does not prescribe one universal number of concepts; the lesson's two-to-four-block range is an explicitly labelled drafting heuristic.

claims.statement: A bibliographic database, a full-text reference collection, a systematic review, a primary research report, and a guideline perform different information functions, so the desired answer product should guide the starting source choice.
claims.scope: A foundation-level starting heuristic for biomedical and health-science information seeking, not a quality hierarchy or exhaustive taxonomy.
claims.uncertainty: A complex or interdisciplinary question may require several source types, databases, grey-literature routes, and an information specialist rather than one starting point.

claims.statement: PubMed searches can use ordinary terms and can map terms to Medical Subject Headings, the vocabulary NLM uses to describe articles; inspecting variants helps represent a concept despite wording differences.
claims.scope: PubMed topic searching and introductory concept planning; this lesson does not teach comprehensive controlled-vocabulary search construction.
claims.uncertainty: Automatic mappings, indexing status, vocabularies, and preferred terms change, and not every relevant record is fully indexed with MeSH.

claims.statement: PubMed may translate or add terms to a query, exposes that translation in Search Details, and returns result patterns that can be used to decide which concept block to revise.
claims.scope: Iterative prototype searching in the current PubMed interface; formal reproducibility and comprehensive search validation are deferred to PREM-02.03.
claims.uncertainty: Result ranking and interface behavior can change, so a future review must verify the current guide and interface.

claims.statement: Neither inclusion in PubMed, PubMed Central, or Bookshelf nor ranking in PubMed constitutes NLM endorsement or a judgment that an individual item is accurate or high quality.
claims.scope: The NLM literature databases covered by the current PubMed disclaimer.

terms.definition: A bounded statement of what a learner needs to know and why the answer will be used.
The form of information needed now, such as an orientation, synthesis, original report, recommendation, or set of candidate citations.
One distinct idea that relevant results should represent, together with terms that express that same idea.
An ordinary-language word or phrase entered to retrieve records containing or mapped from that expression.
A maintained set of preferred subject terms used to describe and retrieve content consistently; MeSH is the NLM example introduced here.
A searchable collection of citation records and descriptive metadata that helps locate documents but is not itself one document type or a quality verdict.
A report presenting the methods and observations of an original study rather than primarily synthesizing several prior studies.
An evidence-synthesis document that addresses a defined question using explicit methods to locate, select, appraise, and combine relevant studies.
A dated recommendation document issued for a specified population, setting, or jurisdiction and intended to guide decisions within its stated scope.
The database-readable interpretation produced from the terms and operators a searcher entered.

attributionHtml:
Attribution

PREM-LPP-006, version 0.1.0, was prepared for EmbeddedKnowledge under the accountable GitHub principal VSBDev and is offered under CC BY 4.0. This lesson is a draft with source confidence pending independent review. It is not reviewed, adjudicated, published, or evidence of curriculum coverage.

The instructional model, prose, fictional cases, concept-block representations, worked example, practice tasks, assessment items, feedback, answer logic, and glossary were newly created for this lesson. The pack contains no third-party media, quotation, adapted expression, or dataset extraction.

Source use and rights

The NCBI/NLM PubMed User Guide, NLM MeSH training course, NCBI Bookshelf Overview, NLM publication-type scope notes, and NCBI/NLM PubMed Disclaimer were used only as factual references. The applicable NLM/NCBI policies identify U.S.-government-created webpages as public-domain information while warning that third-party publications in NLM databases can retain separate copyright. This pack did not process or reuse any deposited book or article expression. Exact locators, dates, rights evidence, source limitations, and agent-access checks appear in references.json.

Agent disclosure

Material authoring assistance came from Codex, provider OpenAI, model GPT-5, through Codex CLI 0.144.6, run ID codex-author-3a467cb9-06e5-40a0-8f83-83a35e0aa976.

The recorded digest is sha256:e2bd6dc3da80a0323684726f0a5cbb34942c6afaf8b2998efd84d7eda91b393d. It covers the exact UTF-8 concatenation described in AUTHORING-BRIEF.md: the delegated task text, the repository AGENTS.md bytes, and the author-role skill bytes with disclosed labels and separators. It excludes hidden provider instructions.

Agent provenance is disclosed and attested by the accountable principal; it is not independently verified and does not count as a review.
